summary: DHH reflects on the announcement that RailsConf 2025 will be the last RailsConf, framing it as a positive transition rather than a loss. He argues that with The Rails Foundation now organizing Rails World (which sold out in twenty minutes), Ruby Central can focus on its core Ruby mission while the Foundation handles Rails-specific advocacy. He reminisces about the early explosive growth of RailsConf from 400 attendees to 1,800, and the memorable keynotes he delivered. He briefly alludes to pandemic-era controversies but pivots to optimism about Rails' future as a counter to ZIRP-era complexity.
key_points: RailsConf 2025 will be the final RailsConf, replaced by Rails World under The Rails Foundation
The split clarifies responsibilities: Ruby Central handles Ruby/RubyGems/Bundler, The Rails Foundation handles Rails
Rails World sold out its thousand-attendee conference in under twenty minutes, proving strong demand
The post-ZIRP complexity hangover makes Rails' simplicity philosophy more relevant than ever
DHH frames pandemic-era community controversies as a catalyst that helped motivate creating The Rails Foundation
money_quotes: We bootstrapped something from nothing, turned it into an epoch-defining event.
The world has woken up from the ZIRP years with a complexity hang-over, and Rails is the perfect painkiller.
The ideal of the one-person framework has never been more relevant.
We never went away, but the renaissance is palpable none the less.
That kind of nonsense is thankfully now largely behind us, not just in the Ruby world, but in tech in general.

Let's go out with a bang in 2025, celebrate the legacy, and then keep plucking away on spreading the joy of beautiful code, incredible productivity, and programmer-centric development with Ruby on Rails.
key_insight: The end of RailsConf isn't a decline but an institutional maturation, with The Rails Foundation taking the reins at the precise moment Rails' simplicity philosophy is most needed in a post-ZIRP tech landscape.
